scriptText:
I. THE LAST BALCONY

[[STAGING]]
[[GLOBAL]]
Generate tearjerker and heartbreaking drama sequence following the provided screenplay. Ensure the result is cinematic, with strong composition and framing. 

Scene Setup:
Against the backdrop of a fading 1970s Istanbul skyline, the world burns quietly. The sky is streaked with the soft, blurred trails of descending meteors—a beautiful, silent herald of the end. Mark and Rosa sit on the jagged concrete lip of an apartment roof, their legs dangling, caught between the gravity of their shared childhood and the weightless of the future. The warmth of the golden hour clashes with the cold reality of their final seconds.
[[/GLOBAL]]
[[LOOKBOOK]]
PART 1: SCENE HEADER (The Lookbook)

SCENE: The Last Balcony
The Aesthetic: Vintage Melancholy (Grainy 16mm feel, warm ambers vs. bruised purples).
Theme/Intention: Reconnection at the precipice of extinction.
Location: Rooftop edge of a Brutalist apartment block, Istanbul.
Time: DUSK (The Blue Hour).
Lighting: Backlit by a low-hanging sun. Practical "meteor" light flickers (warm/cyan) reflecting off skin.
Sound Atmosphere: Low-frequency wind hum, rising chaotic city sirens, muffled shouting, and car horns from the streets below.
Key Props/Set Pieces: Worn leather shoes dangling over the edge.
Continuity State: Ash on Mark’s sleeve; Rosa’s hair windswept.
VFX Notes: Heavy Bokeh on the background sky to blur the meteor shower into abstract light streaks.

CHARACTER
@image Mark: 1970s tailored corduroy jacket, dark turtleneck. Sideburns, hair slightly disheveled.
@image Rosa: High-waisted patterned trousers, mustard knit top. Hoop earrings. Soft beehive hairstyle.

STAGING & FLOW
Visual Blocking: Mark and Rosa sit shoulder-to-shoulder, no physical contact. Their feet kick rhythmically at the air.
Shot Map: Begins with an Extreme Wide to emphasize isolation. Handheld camera movement throughout to create a sense of instability and raw emotion.
[[/LOOKBOOK]]
[[/STAGING]]
EXT. APARTMENT ROOFTOP - DUSK

[SHOT 1: EWS - LOW ANGLE, HANDHELD]
The massive silhouette of the apartment building against a bruised sky. Two tiny figures sit on the ledge. Streaks of light (VFX) cut through the clouds.
SFX: DISTANT SIRENS AND CITY CHAOS SWELL.

ROSA (O.S.):
Looks like the courtyard.

MARK (O.S.):
The stars are falling now.

[SHOT 2: OTS MARK - MS, HANDHELD]
Mark gazes at the horizon, a faint, sad smile on his lips. He turns slowly to Rosa.

[SHOT 3: OTS ROSA - MS, HANDHELD]
Rosa’s eyes are glassy, reflecting the fire in the sky. She holds back tears.

MARK:
I still owe you that dinner.

[SHOT 4: CU ROSA - EYE LEVEL, HANDHELD]
Rosa glares at him with a cynical, watery-eyed smirk.

ROSA:
(Sarcastic)
Fifteen years, Mark. Right on time.

[SHOT 5: CU MARK - EYE LEVEL, HANDHELD]
Mark lets out a dry, soft laugh. He looks back at the sky.

MARK:
Just watch. It’s on the house.

[SHOT 6: MCU - LOW ANGLE, HANDHELD PUSH-IN]
Mark slowly closes his eyes.
Rosa turns her head, looking at Mark with a gaze of quiet admiration and awe.
A beat.
Rosa slowly closes her eyes as well, leaning into the wind.
SFX: THE SIRENS BLEND INTO A SINGLE HIGH-FREQUENCY WHINE.

II. THE WITNESS

[[STAGING]]
[[GLOBAL]]
Generate Intense Dramatic Scene following the provided screenplay. Ensure the result is cinematic, with strong composition and framing.
[[/GLOBAL]]
[[LOOKBOOK]]
PART 1: SCENE HEADER (The Lookbook)

SCENE
The Aesthetic: "The Fluidity of Fear." A single, relentless movement that feels like a noose tightening.
Theme/Intention: Coerced Complicity. The camera acts as an invisible observer trapped in the room with them.
Location: Guest Room (Stripped bare, echoing).
Time: NIGHT
Lighting: High-contrast "Rembrandt" lighting. A single warm source from the hallway is cut off when the door slams, leaving only a cold, moonlight blue from the window.
Sound Atmosphere: Dead silence, punctuated by the rhythmic, wet sound of heavy breathing. The house feels like it’s listening.
Key Props/Set Pieces: A heavy mahogany door; a window reflecting the internal storm.
Continuity State: The sound of tires on gravel has just vanished.
VFX Notes: Handheld Steadicam for a "floating but frantic" perspective.

CHARACTER
@image1 Robert: 45. In a dark wool overcoat. He looks heavy, imposing, and exhausted.
@image2 Rosa: 30. Barefoot, in a silk robe. She looks fragile, her posture collapsing inward.

STAGING & FLOW
Visual Blocking: Robert LURCHES into the room, physically pulling Rosa in by the arm. He DRIVES the door shut with his body weight. Rosa RECOILS toward the window, her body language a series of sharp, defensive angles.
Shot Map: THE CONTINUOUS ONER. The camera follows Robert’s back (POV-adjacent), then SWINGS around him to catch Rosa’s face. It then ORBITS them during the banter, narrowing the space between them until it’s a tight "Two-Shot" profile, ending on a slow PUSH-IN to Rosa’s eyes.
[[/LOOKBOOK]]
[[/STAGING]]

INT. GUEST ROOM - NIGHT

[SHOT 1: MS TO ECU - ARC, TRACKING / CONTINUOUS TAKE]
The door EXPLODES open. ROBERT (45) hauls ROSA (30) inside. He SLAMS the door shut—the frame SHAKES. He stays pinned against the wood, blocking the exit.

ROBERT:
The driveway, Rosa. Again.

The camera SWINGS around Robert's shoulder to find Rosa. She’s trembling, her back hitting the cold window glass.

ROSA:
(A panicked whisper)
She said... she said we didn't need her.

Robert LUNGES forward, closing the six-foot gap in a second. The camera PITCHES with him, landing in a claustrophobic profile.

ROBERT:
(Overlapping)
Stop. He’s gasping for air and you’re just reciting her scripts.

ROSA:
(Desperate)
It’s her house, Rob! I have nowhere—!

ROBERT:
(Cutting her off, a cold snarl)
You’re not a daughter anymore. You’re a witness.

The camera DRIFTS closer, tightening on Rosa’s face. The anger in her eyes dies, replaced by a hollow, terrifying void. She looks past him at the door.

ROSA:
(Voice breaking)
He won’t even fight for himself...

The camera CREEPS into an ECU of Rosa’s eyes. A tear tracks through her makeup.

ROSA:
Why am I the only one dying in this house?

Robert’s reflection in the window behind her remains frozen. His voice is a low, devastating hum.

ROBERT (O.S.):
Because you’re the only one she has left to break.

The camera holds on Rosa’s shattering expression as Robert’s shadow RECEDES from the wall behind her.

III. THE LAST WEIGHT

[[STAGING]]
[[GLOBAL]]
Generate tearjerker and heartbreaking continuous take oner scene following the provided screenplay. Ensure the result is cinematic, with strong composition and framing.

Budapest, 1956. The air is thick with the caustic smell of pulverized limestone and cordite. Amidst the skeletal remains of a residential block, the silence is broken by the rhythmic crunch of boots on glass and the distant, hollow echo of sirens. @7d25b8e9-226a-4281-af72-b4a6dbb00e8f MARK, a man transformed into a ghost by a coating of white masonry dust, wages a frantic, losing war against a mountain of brick that has swallowed his family. The camera, trembling like a frightened witness from the perspective of a fellow survivor, captures the raw, unpolished agony of a man realizing he is the only one left breathing in a graveyard of his own making.
[[/GLOBAL]]
[[LOOKBOOK]]
SCENE: The Last Weight.
The Aesthetic: "The Ash of Memory." Desaturated, gritty, high-contrast monochrome tones in a color space (muted blues and ochres).
Theme/Intention: The isolation of survival. To make the audience feel the physical and emotional weight of "being the only one left."
Location: Exterior - Ruined Apartment Complex, Budapest (Post-WWII/Revolution style).
Time: DUSK. Flat, dying light filtered through a thick haze of smoke.
Lighting: Naturalistic, overcast sky (6500K). High-side key light to emphasize the texture of the dust on Mark’s face. Deep shadows in the rubble.
Sound Atmosphere: Low-frequency rumble of distant artillery. The rhythmic, sharp clink of stones being moved. Mark’s ragged, wheezing breath (hyper-focused).
Continuity State: Mark has just broken away from the triage line; his hands are bloodied under the dust.

CHARACTER
@image1 Mark: (35) Full, matted beard caked with grey ash. Wearing a torn, high-waisted wool suit trouser and a white linen shirt now stained charcoal. No coat. Eyes bloodshot, pupils dilated.
@image2 Robert: (55) Rescuer/Civil Defense. Period-accurate "White Helmet" (steel M42 style, painted white). Heavy navy wool utility coat with a faded armband. Stern but empathetic face, deeply lined.

STAGING & FLOW
Visual Blocking: Mark moves in a jagged, erratic line toward the "epicenter" (the rubble). He is kinetic and desperate. Robert moves in a slow, steady arc, attempting to intercept Mark’s momentum without triggering a flight response.
Shot Map: An Oner. The camera follows Mark from a 3-meter distance, staying at eye level, mimicking a person following him through the debris. The distance closes only when Robert enters the frame to create a suffocating three-shot.
[[/LOOKBOOK]]
[[/STAGING]]

EXT. RUINED APARTMENT COMPLEX - DUSK

[SHOT 1: WS to MS - EYE LEVEL, HANDHELD/ONER]
The camera sits wide, revealing a smoking graveyard of masonry. A small, white figure—MARK—stumbles from an ambulance into the center of the frame. The camera PUSHES IN and SHAKES, tracking him as he reaches the rubble.

MARK:
(Breathless, guttural)
No. No.

He drops to his knees, clawing at a heavy slab. His bloodied fingers leave red streaks on the white dust. He stops, looking back at the SILENT CROWD of survivors. He locks eyes with ROBERT.

MARK:
Help me! Do something!

Robert steps forward into the frame, his shadow falling over Mark. He places a heavy, steadying hand on Mark’s trembling shoulder.

ROBERT:
Mark. It’s finished.

Mark’s chest hitches—a sharp, wheezing ASMA ATTACK. He looks up at Robert, his expression shattering. He looks at the silent rubble.

MARK:
It’s... so quiet.

Mark’s hands go limp, sliding off the stone. He stares into the void of the debris. Robert gently pulls him away.

IV. BROKEN

[[STAGING]]
[[GLOBAL]]
Generate drama continuous take oner scene following the provided screenplay. Ensure the result is cinematic, with strong composition and framing.
[[/GLOBAL]]
[[LOOKBOOK]]
SCENE: The Weight of Silence
The Aesthetic: Tropical Colonial Noir. High contrast, humid atmosphere, and heavy textures (wood vs. linen).
Theme/Intention: The collapse of stoicism. To show the transition from volatile rage to shared vulnerability.
Location: High-ceiling Colonial Office (Batavia). Dark mahogany walls, tall teak shutters.
Time: DAY (Harsh afternoon sun cutting through slats).
Lighting: Chiaroscuro effect. Natural light filtered through wooden blinds (blinds create "jail bar" shadows). Warm, dim interior (3200K) vs. blown-out tropical exterior (5600K) seen through door gaps.
Sound Atmosphere: Rhythmic THUMP-THUMP of a slow-moving ceiling fan. Distant street sounds (horse carriages). Muffled shouting from the hallway. Sharp CRACK of wood hitting wood.
Key Props/Set Pieces: Large mahogany desk, heavy brass inkwell, white plaster arm cast (heavily soiled/worn), silk sling.
Continuity State: Mark’s arm is in a rigid, slightly dirty cast (left). Robert is impeccably groomed until the physical contact.
VFX Notes: Dust particles visible in light shafts (SFX Haze).

CHARACTER
@image1 Mark: 35. Javanese Nobleman attire. Fine batik sarong with a dark European-style tailored vest. Left arm in a thick white plaster cast. Hair is disheveled, face is sweaty and flushed.
@image2 Robert: 55. High-ranking Resident Official. Formal white linen "Baju Tutup" with high collar and gold brass buttons. Polished black boots. Hair slicked back, rigid posture.

STAGING & FLOW
Visual Blocking: Robert starts static at the window (Background). Mark enters from the Z-axis (Foreground) and aggressively closes the distance. Mark’s movements are jagged and unstable due to his injury. Robert remains a pillar until Mark physically collapses into his space.
Shot Map: A single continuous HANDHELD sequence. The camera follows Mark’s "charge" into the room, orbiting the two men as they exchange words, ending in a tight, breathing Close-Up (CU) on the final embrace.
[[/LOOKBOOK]]
[[/STAGING]]

INT. COLONIAL OFFICE - DAY

[SHOT 1: MS - EYE LEVEL, HANDHELD]
The camera stays on ROBERT. He stands with his back to us, looking through the wooden shutters. He is a statue in white linen. From the hallway, we hear a MUFFLED SHOUT and a SCUFFLE.

The heavy doors CRASH open (SFX: WOODEN IMPACT). MARK bursts in, chest heaving. The camera WHIPS to catch him. He is a mess of batik and plaster. He kicks a small side chair (SFX: CLATTER).

MARK:
(Breathless, voice cracking)
You knew. You sat here and you knew!

The camera circles Mark as he storms toward the desk. Robert turns slowly. He is calm, which only fuels Mark’s fire.

ROBERT:
Your arm, Mark. You’ll break the cast.

MARK:
(Overlapping)
Three days! He was gone for three days! You let me eat... you let me...

ROBERT:
(Cutting in)
I did it to protect you! You were in no state—

MARK:
(Screaming)
I should have been the one!

Mark reaches Robert. His right hand grabs Robert’s gold buttons. His left arm (the cast) is pinned between them, awkward and heavy. Mark’s face is a mask of agony. He’s trying to hold back a sob, but his breath comes out in jagged shudders.

MARK:
(A broken whisper)
He was my brother.

The camera moves in tight on Robert’s face. His stoic expression shatters. His eyes well up. He reaches out and pulls Mark into a crushing embrace. Robert’s hand goes to the back of Mark’s head, stroking his hair with a fatherly rhythm.

MARK:
(Guttural sob)
Uuuggghhh—!

Mark’s weight fully drops into Robert. Robert’s eyes close, a single tear cutting through his composure. The camera stays close, catching the sound of heavy sobbing and the rhythmic creak of the ceiling fan.

V. BLUE HOUR

[[STAGING]]
[[GLOBAL]]
Generate an emotional scene based on this screenplay. Ensure the result is cinematic, with strong composition and framing.
[[/GLOBAL]]
[[/STAGING]]

EXT. RURAL ROAD - TWILIGHT

WIDE SHOT. The wind whips SARAH’S hair across her face. She looks exhausted, her shoulders hunched against the cold. ELIAS is a static statue, staring down the infinite, empty road.

SARAH:
(Voice strained, shivering)
We’ve been out here for an hour. She isn't—

[MEDIUM SHOT - OVER SARAH'S SHOULDER.]
ELIAS doesn’t move his head, but his eyes snap toward her, sharp and defensive.

ELIAS:
(Cutting her off)
I said wait.

[CLOSE UP - SARAH.]
She steps into his space, invading his "grief bubble." Her expression is a painful mix of anger and heartbreak. Tears are visible but they aren't falling; they are frozen by the wind.

SARAH:
Wait for what? The wind to say her name? It’s just us, Dad. Just us and this road.

[MEDIUM CLOSE UP - THE INTERACTION.]
Elias’s head finally drops. He looks small, his denim jacket hanging off his slumped shoulders.

ELIAS:
(A broken whisper)
One more minute.

SARAH:
(Firmly)
A minute won't bring her back.

Sarah reaches out and GRABS his hand with a firm, desperate tug. She begins to pull him toward the camera.

SARAH:
Walk with me, or I’m leaving you here.

VI. THE BRIDGE

[[STAGING]]
[[GLOBAL]]
Generate an emotional scene based on this screenplay. Ensure the result is cinematic, with strong composition and framing.
[[/GLOBAL]]
[[VISUAL BLOCKING]]
VISUAL BLOCKING
- MICK (Right Foreground): Hunched over the wheel. His hands are a blur as he fumbles with the ignition.
- LEO (Center): Pinioned between the two men. His head is on a swivel, reacting to the interior (Mick) and exterior (Visitor) simultaneously.
- THE VISITOR (Left, through Window): His face is distorted against the glass, his breath fogging the window as he screams.
[[/VISUAL BLOCKING]]
[[/STAGING]]

INT. SEDAN - DAY (OVERCAST/RAIN)

[SHOT 1: MEDIUM INTERIOR (ESTABLISHING)]
Mick TURNS THE KEY. The engine COUGHS—whir-whir-click—but fails to catch. The Visitor SLAMS his palm against the driver-side glass. Rain streaks his face like tears.

VISITOR:
(Muffled, frantic)
Mick! Don’t take the bridge!

LEO:
Mick, who is that?

[SHOT 2: CLOSE UP - MICK]
Mick’s jaw is locked. He RIPS the key back and turns it again. The starter MOTOR WHINES. He ignores the man outside entirely, eyes fixed on the dashboard.

MICK:
Don’t look at him.

VISITOR (O.S.):
Mick! Look at me! Take the dirt road!

[SHOT 3: CLOSE UP - LEO]
Leo is hyperventilating.

LEO:
How does he know your name?

MICK (O.S.):
Lock the door, Leo.

LEO:
He’s crying! He looks just like—

[SHOT 4: MEDIUM INTERIOR / CAR EXTERIOR]
Mick BARK-SHOUTS over the sound of the failing engine. He POUNDS the steering wheel just as the V8 FINALLY ROARS TO LIFE.

MICK:
I know what he looks like!

VISITOR:
(Screaming)
THE BRIDGE IS OUT! Turn Around!

Mick SLAMS the car into gear. The tires CHIRP on the wet gravel.

MICK:
Hold on.

LEO:
Mick, wait—!

The car LUNGES forward. The Visitor’s hand SLIDES off the glass in the rain.

CUT TO BLACK.
